comments.body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**`mockHttp` reports the wrong search `total_count`, weakening cap-selection coverage.**

On Line 67, `total_count` is derived from `canned.searchItems.length` (page size), not total candidate count. This can make first-sync cap tests pass for the wrong reason when simulating `> 20` candidates.

 

<details>
<summary>Suggested fix</summary>

```diff
 interface CannedRouting {
 	searchItems: PRFixture[];
 	prs: PRFixture[];
+	searchTotalCount?: number;
 	rateLimitOnPR?: number;
 }
@@
 		if (url.includes("/search/issues")) {
 			return respond({
-				total_count: canned.searchItems.length,
+				total_count: canned.searchTotalCount ?? canned.prs.length,
 				items: canned.searchItems.map(searchItem),
 			});
 		}
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.test.ts` around lines 65 - 69, The mock
response is returning total_count as the page size (canned.searchItems.length)
which misrepresents the real candidate total; change the total_count to use a
dedicated total field (e.g. canned.searchTotal or canned.searchTotalCount — add
this field to the canned data fixtures if missing) instead of
canned.searchItems.length so that mockHttp's "/search/issues" response
accurately reflects the full candidate count; update the code that maps items
(items: canned.searchItems.map(searchItem)) to remain unchanged while ensuring
tests that simulate "> 20" candidates rely on the new canned.searchTotal value.
```

</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`GitHub REST API: for HTTP 403 responses, which headers/body fields distinguish rate limiting (primary/secondary) from auth/permission failures?`

💡 Result:

When the GitHub REST API returns an HTTP 403 Forbidden response, you can distinguish between primary rate limits, secondary rate limits, and permission/authentication failures by examining the response headers and the JSON body message [1][2][3]. Primary Rate Limiting Primary rate limits are based on your hourly quota. When exceeded, the API returns a 403 (or 429) status code [1][4]. - Headers: The x-ratelimit-remaining header will be 0 [1][2]. You should check the x-ratelimit-reset header, which provides the UTC epoch time when your quota will reset [1][5]. - Body: The JSON response typically contains a message indicating that the API rate limit has been exceeded [6][7]. Secondary Rate Limiting Secondary rate limits are abuse-prevention measures triggered by high concurrency, rapid mutation operations, or CPU-intensive requests [3][7]. - Headers: If the Retry-After header is present, it indicates the number of seconds you must wait before retrying [1][4]. Note that this header is not always present for secondary rate limits [3]. - Body: The JSON response will explicitly include an error message stating that you have exceeded a secondary rate limit [1][2]. Permission and Authentication Failures If a 403 error is not related to rate limiting, it is likely a permission or scope issue [1][7]. - Headers: You can use the X-Accepted-GitHub-Permissions header to identify the specific permissions required for the endpoint you are attempting to access [1][8]. - Body: The JSON response will contain a message such as Resource not accessible by integration or Resource not accessible by personal access token [1][7]. Summary Table Error Type | Key Indicator --- | --- Primary Rate Limit | x-ratelimit-remaining is 0 [1][2] Secondary Rate Limit | Body message mentions secondary rate limit [1][2] Permission Failure | Body message mentions Resource not accessible; check X-Accepted-GitHub-Permissions [1][2]

Citations:

- 1: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/overview/troubleshooting
- 2: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/using-the-rest-api/troubleshooting-the-rest-api
- 3: https://errormedic.com/api/github-api/github-api-rate-limit-errors-fix-401-403-429-502-timeouts
- 4: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/using-the-rest-api/rate-limits-for-the-rest-api
- 5: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/overview/rate-limits-for-the-rest-api
- 6: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38378337/receiving-github-api-403-error-when-i-have-not-exceeded-my-rate-limit
- 7: https://errormedic.com/api/github-api/github-api-rate-limit-errors-fix-401-403-429-502-timeout-2024-guide
- 8: https://docs.github.com/en/enterprise-server@3.19/rest/using-the-rest-api/troubleshooting-the-rest-api?apiVersion=2022-11-28

</details>

**Distinguish 403 from rate-limit using documented headers.**

Currently, any 403 response is treated as a rate-limit hit after checking the remaining header, which misclassifies auth/permission failures and masks configuration issues. According to GitHub API documentation, primary rate limits set `x-ratelimit-remaining: "0"` and secondary rate limits include a `Retry-After` header. Only these indicate actual rate-limiting; other 403s are auth/permission failures.

<details>
<summary>Suggested fix</summary>

```diff
 function isRateLimited(res: HttpResponse): boolean {
-	if (res.status !== 403 && res.status !== 429) return false;
-	if (res.headers["x-ratelimit-remaining"] === "0") return true;
-	// Secondary rate limit responses use 403 without a 0 remaining counter.
-	return true;
+	if (res.status === 429) return true;
+	if (res.status !== 403) return false;
+
+	const remaining = res.headers["x-ratelimit-remaining"];
+	const retryAfter = res.headers["retry-after"];
+	// 403 can be auth/permission-related; only treat known RL indicators as rate-limit.
+	return remaining === "0" || retryAfter !== undefined;
 }
```
</details>

<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
function isRateLimited(res: HttpResponse): boolean {
	if (res.status === 429) return true;
	if (res.status !== 403) return false;

	const remaining = res.headers["x-ratelimit-remaining"];
	const retryAfter = res.headers["retry-after"];
	// 403 can be auth/permission-related; only treat known RL indicators as rate-limit.
	return remaining === "0" || retryAfter !== undefined;
}
```

</details>

<!-- suggestion_end -->

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.ts` around lines 79 - 84, The current
isRateLimited(HttpResponse) incorrectly treats all 403s as rate limits; change
it so 429 always returns true, 403 only returns true when the
"x-ratelimit-remaining" header === "0" OR when a "retry-after" header is present
(indicating secondary rate limiting), and otherwise return false (so other 403s
are treated as auth/permission failures). Update the logic in the isRateLimited
function to check these specific headers rather than blanket-true for 403.
```

</details>

[![Review Change Stack](https://storage.googleapis.com/coderabbit_public_assets/review-stack-in-coderabbit-ui.svg)](https://app.coderabbit.ai/change-stack/cesarnml/codogotchi/pull/11?utm_source=github_walkthrough&utm_medium=github&utm_campaign=change_stack)

<!-- review_stack_entry_end -->
<!-- walkthrough_start -->

## Walkthrough

This PR introduces a new GitHub PR signal source module to the engine package. It defines exported type contracts for HTTP abstraction and signal shapes, implements HTTP fetching with rate-limit detection and defensive JSON parsing for GitHub search and PR detail endpoints, provides a bounded concurrency runner to enrich PR details while respecting rate limits, and implements the main `readGithubSignals` orchestration function. The implementation includes first-sync cap selection to enforce a 20-PR limit via a single search query with a 90-day merge date floor, scoring via a separate `scorePR` function, and graceful rate-limit handling that returns partial results with a flag rather than throwing. Comprehensive test coverage validates cap selection, first and subsequent sync behavior, enrichment and scoring including revert-title collapse and review-comment-based score reduction, and rate-limit scenarios. Documentation captures key behavioral decisions, and the module is exported via barrel export in the engine package entry point.

<!-- walkthrough_end -->

🧹 Nitpick comments (2)
packages/engine/src/sources/github.test.ts (2)
`71-84`: _⚡ Quick win_ **PR detail fixture matching should include repository identity, not only PR number.** Line 82 matches by `number` only. If fixtures ever include multiple repos, duplicate PR numbers can resolve to the wrong detail payload and produce misleading scoring assertions.
Suggested fix ```diff - const m = url.match(/\/repos\/[^/]+\/[^/]+\/pulls\/(\d+)/); + const m = url.match(/\/repos\/([^/]+)\/([^/]+)\/pulls\/(\d+)/); if (m) { - const num = Number(m[1]); + const fullName = `${m[1]}/${m[2]}`; + const num = Number(m[3]); if (canned.rateLimitOnPR === num) { return { @@ - const pr = canned.prs.find((p) => p.number === num); + const pr = canned.prs.find( + (p) => p.number === num && p.repoFullName === fullName, + ); if (!pr) return respond({ message: "Not Found" }, 404); return respond(prDetail(pr)); } ```
🤖 Prompt for AI Agents ``` Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate. In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.test.ts` around lines 71 - 84, The PR detail lookup in the test stub currently matches only by PR number (using url.match -> num and then canned.prs.find(p => p.number === num)), which can return the wrong PR when fixtures contain multiple repositories with duplicate numbers; update the lookup to also consider repository identity by parsing owner/repo from the URL (from the same url.match or a new regex) and then find the PR using both p.number === num and p.base.repo.full_name (or equivalent repo identifying fields) === `${owner}/${repo}` before returning prDetail(pr) or respond; ensure the rate-limit check (canned.rateLimitOnPR) still uses the parsed num. ```
--- `91-107`: _⚡ Quick win_ **Add an explicit boundary test for `candidateCount === perPageLimit` to lock cap semantics.** The suite covers `< 20` and `> 20`, but not the equality boundary. A single assertion here prevents off-by-one regressions in `applyFirstSyncCap`.
🤖 Prompt for AI Agents ``` Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate. In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.test.ts` around lines 91 - 107, Add a unit test for the boundary case where candidateCount === perPageLimit to ensure applyFirstSyncCap returns the intended arm; specifically add a new it block that calls applyFirstSyncCap({ candidateCount: X, perPageLimit: X }) (e.g., 20 and 20) and asserts the expected cap string (match either "last-20" or "ninety-day" according to current semantics) so the equality case is locked and prevents off-by-one regressions; reference applyFirstSyncCap, candidateCount and perPageLimit when locating where to add the test. ```
🤖 Prompt for all review comments with AI agents ``` Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate. Inline comments: In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.test.ts`: - Around line 65-69: The mock response is returning total_count as the page size (canned.searchItems.length) which misrepresents the real candidate total; change the total_count to use a dedicated total field (e.g. canned.searchTotal or canned.searchTotalCount — add this field to the canned data fixtures if missing) instead of canned.searchItems.length so that mockHttp's "/search/issues" response accurately reflects the full candidate count; update the code that maps items (items: canned.searchItems.map(searchItem)) to remain unchanged while ensuring tests that simulate "> 20" candidates rely on the new canned.searchTotal value. In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.ts`: - Around line 241-250: The fetch and JSON parsing steps around http(searchUrl, { headers }) and parseSearchItems(await searchRes.json()) can throw and currently bubble up; wrap both the HTTP call and the JSON parsing in a try/catch so thrown errors are caught and the function returns the graceful fallback (the existing empty-like response with rateLimitHit/other flags) instead of crashing the whole sync; specifically, in the block that uses searchUrl/searchRes/items and the similar block at the other location (lines 269-280 equivalent) catch errors from http(...) and from searchRes.json(), debugLog the error (including context like searchUrl and phase), and return the same empty response (or { ...empty, fetchError: true }) so enrichment lanes can continue. - Around line 79-84: The current isRateLimited(HttpResponse) incorrectly treats all 403s as rate limits; change it so 429 always returns true, 403 only returns true when the "x-ratelimit-remaining" header === "0" OR when a "retry-after" header is present (indicating secondary rate limiting), and otherwise return false (so other 403s are treated as auth/permission failures). Update the logic in the isRateLimited function to check these specific headers rather than blanket-true for 403. --- Nitpick comments: In `@packages/engine/src/sources/github.test.ts`: - Around line 71-84: The PR detail lookup in the test stub currently matches only by PR number (using url.match -> num and then canned.prs.find(p => p.number === num)), which can return the wrong PR when fixtures contain multiple repositories with duplicate numbers; update the lookup to also consider repository identity by parsing owner/repo from the URL (from the same url.match or a new regex) and then find the PR using both p.number === num and p.base.repo.full_name (or equivalent repo identifying fields) === `${owner}/${repo}` before returning prDetail(pr) or respond; ensure the rate-limit check (canned.rateLimitOnPR) still uses the parsed num. - Around line 91-107: Add a unit test for the boundary case where candidateCount === perPageLimit to ensure applyFirstSyncCap returns the intended arm; specifically add a new it block that calls applyFirstSyncCap({ candidateCount: X, perPageLimit: X }) (e.g., 20 and 20) and asserts the expected cap string (match either "last-20" or "ninety-day" according to current semantics) so the equality case is locked and prevents off-by-one regressions; reference applyFirstSyncCap, candidateCount and perPageLimit when locating where to add the test. ```
